bytes.title: Kafka Queues — Decoupling, Async Processing, and Load Leveling Explained
bytes.body:
Kafka decouples producers from consumers by writing events to a durable, partitioned log — producers never wait for downstream processing. A payment service publishing to a `transactions` topic has zero knowledge of the fraud-detection consumer reading it; deploy, restart, or scale either side independently.

Async processing drops synchronous latency from your hot path. Instead of calling a notification service inline, publish a `UserSignedUp` event and return HTTP 201 immediately. The consumer processes it on its own schedule.

Load leveling is where Kafka's retention model shines. A burst of 50,000 events/sec can be absorbed into partitions and consumed at a sustainable 5,000 events/sec — the log acts as a shock absorber. Unlike RabbitMQ queues, messages aren't lost after ACK; a consumer can lag behind and catch up without backpressure cascading upstream.

Partition count directly controls max parallelism: 12 partitions → max 12 active consumers in a group. Set `max.poll.records` and `fetch.max.bytes` to tune throughput vs. latency tradeoffs per consumer group.

bytes.topic: Message Delivery Guarantees — at-least-once vs at-most-once vs exactly-once
bytes.title: Kafka Message Delivery Guarantees — At-Most-Once, At-Least-Once, and Exactly-Once Explained
bytes.body:
Kafka's delivery semantics are controlled by a combination of producer configs, consumer commit strategies, and broker settings — not a single flag.

**At-most-once**: Set `acks=0` on the producer and commit offsets *before* processing. Messages can be lost if the broker crashes or processing fails, but you'll never reprocess.

**At-least-once**: Use `acks=all` with `retries > 0` and commit offsets *after* processing. Duplicates are possible on retry — your consumer logic must be idempotent.

**Exactly-once (EOS)**: Requires `enable.idempotence=true` on the producer (assigns a PID + sequence number, deduped by the broker) and `isolation.level=read_committed` on the consumer. For cross-partition atomicity, use the Kafka Transactions API: `initTransactions()`, `beginTransaction()`, `commitTransaction()`. EOS has ~10–20% throughput overhead and requires Kafka 2.5+ with a 3-broker quorum. In Kafka Streams, set `processing.guarantee=exactly_once_v2` — it wires all of this automatically.

bytes.topic: Dead Letter Queues — poison message handling, retry strategies with backoff
bytes.title: Kafka Dead Letter Queues — Poison Message Handling and Backoff Retry
bytes.body:
Kafka has no native DLQ, so you build one. When a consumer fails to process a message, the naive approach is to `seek` back and retry in a tight loop — this blocks the partition and starves other consumers. The production pattern: use a retry topic chain (`topic.retry-1`, `topic.retry-2`, `topic.dlq`) with exponential backoff encoded as a header.

Publish the failed record to `topic.retry-1` with a `retry-count` and `next-process-after` header (epoch ms). A separate consumer group polls the retry topic but skips records where `next-process-after > System.currentTimeMillis()`, calling `consumer.pause()` + `consumer.seek()` to hold position without spinning. After max retries (e.g. 3), route to the DLQ topic.

Spring Kafka's `DefaultErrorHandler` with `ExponentialBackOffWithMaxRetries` automates this via `SeekToCurrentErrorHandler` under the hood, wiring retry topics through `DeadLetterPublishingRecoverer`. For non-Spring consumers, implement `ConsumerRebalanceListener` to replay DLQ messages on demand — never auto-consume the DLQ, treat it as an audit log first.

bytes.topic: Idempotency — designing consumers to safely handle duplicate messages
bytes.title: Kafka Idempotent Consumers — Safely Handling Duplicate Messages
bytes.body:
Kafka's at-least-once delivery guarantee means your consumer will occasionally process the same message twice — during rebalances, broker failovers, or after a crash before offsets are committed. The safest pattern is to make your processing logic idempotent at the storage layer, not just in memory.

Use the message's topic + partition + offset as a natural idempotency key. Before processing, check a deduplication store (Redis with a short TTL, or a `processed_offsets` Postgres table) for that key. If present, skip and commit. If absent, process and record atomically.

For exactly-once semantics within Kafka itself, enable the transactional API: set `enable.idempotence=true` and `transactional.id` on the producer, then use `beginTransaction()` / `sendOffsetsToTransaction()` / `commitTransaction()` to atomically write output records and commit input offsets in a single transaction. This prevents zombie producers from re-emitting during leader epoch changes.

Avoid relying on `auto.offset.reset=earliest` as a safety net — it's a recovery knob, not an idempotency strategy. Always pair offset management with a durable dedup check.

bytes.topic: Kafka — partitions, consumer groups, offset management, log compaction
bytes.title: Kafka Internals — Partitions, Consumer Groups, and Log Compaction Explained
bytes.body: Each Kafka topic is split into partitions — the unit of parallelism. A partition is an ordered, immutable log; producers append to the tail, consumers read from an offset. Within a consumer group, Kafka assigns each partition to exactly one member, so adding consumers up to the partition count scales throughput linearly. Beyond that, extra consumers sit idle. Offsets are committed to the internal `__consumer_offsets` topic. Use `enable.auto.commit=false` and commit after processing to avoid losing or double-processing messages on rebalance. For at-least-once guarantees, commit post-process; for at-most-once, pre-process. Log compaction (`cleanup.policy=compact`) changes retention semantics: instead of time- or size-based deletion, Kafka retains only the latest record per key, making it ideal for changelog or materialized-view use cases (e.g., Kafka Streams state stores). The cleaner thread runs in the background — `min.cleanable.dirty.ratio` controls how aggressively it compacts. Combine compaction with `delete` policy (`compact,delete`) to support both tombstone deletion and size caps.

bytes.topic: RabbitMQ — exchanges, routing keys, acknowledgements, prefetch count
bytes.title: Kafka Dev's Guide to RabbitMQ: Exchanges, Routing Keys, Acks, and Prefetch
bytes.body:
RabbitMQ's mental model diverges sharply from Kafka's. Where Kafka routes via topic partitions, RabbitMQ splits routing into exchanges and queues. A direct exchange matches exact routing keys; a topic exchange uses wildcard patterns (`logs.*`, `logs.#`). Unlike Kafka's durable, replayable log, messages are consumed-and-gone unless you wire a dead-letter exchange (DLX) for failures.

Acknowledgements are per-message, not offset-based. `basic_ack` permanently removes the message from the broker; `basic_nack(requeue=True)` re-enqueues it. There is no offset rewind — once acked, it's gone. This makes idempotent consumers mandatory in ways Kafka's consumer group offset replay lets you avoid.

Prefetch count (`basic_qos`) caps unacked in-flight messages per consumer — conceptually similar to `max.poll.records`, but enforced broker-side at push time rather than client-side at poll time. Set it too high and a slow consumer buffers a backlog locally; too low and you serialize delivery. For CPU-bound workers, 10–50 is a practical baseline.

The structural shift: Kafka consumers pull on their schedule; RabbitMQ pushes. Prefetch count is your primary backpressure lever.
bytes.code_snippet:
import pika

connection = pika.BlockingConnection(pika.ConnectionParameters('localhost'))
channel = connection.channel()

channel.exchange_declare(exchange='events', exchange_type='topic')
channel.queue_declare(queue='tasks', durable=True)
channel.queue_bind(exchange='events', queue='tasks', routing_key='task.*')
channel.basic_qos(prefetch_count=10)

def on_message(ch, method, properties, body):
    process(body)  # idempotent handler
    ch.basic_ack(delivery_tag=method.delivery_tag)

channel.basic_consume(queue='tasks', on_message_callback=on_message)
channel.start_consuming()

bytes.topic: Redis as Queue — pub/sub vs streams vs lists, persistence trade-offs
bytes.title: Kafka Developer's Guide to Redis Queues — Lists, Pub/Sub, and Streams
bytes.body:
Coming from Kafka, Redis queue primitives can feel stripped-down — but each targets a distinct delivery contract.

**Lists** (`LPUSH`/`BRPOP`) are the simplest: single consumer race, no ACK, no replay. Think Kafka with no consumer groups and retention set to zero. Acceptable for at-most-once job queues where loss is tolerable.

**Pub/Sub** is pure fire-and-forget. No persistence, no buffering — disconnected subscribers simply miss messages. Unlike Kafka's durable commit log, there's no offset to seek. Reserve it for ephemeral signals: cache invalidation, live presence, real-time dashboards.

**Streams** (`XADD`/`XREADGROUP`/`XACK`) are the closest Redis analog to a Kafka topic: named consumer groups, per-consumer pending lists, `XPENDING` to reclaim unacked messages, and `MAXLEN` for bounded retention. The critical difference: Redis Streams persist via AOF/RDB, not a replicated log. Without `appendonly yes`, a broker crash drops your queue entirely — there's no ISR failover. With AOF `always` mode you get near-durable writes at the cost of throughput.

**Rule:** Use Streams when you need consumer groups or replay. Use Lists for simple worker queues. Never use Pub/Sub for anything requiring delivery guarantees.
bytes.code_snippet:
import redis

r = redis.Redis(decode_responses=True)

# Producer
r.xadd("events", {"type": "user.signup", "user_id": "42"})

# Create consumer group (analog to Kafka consumer group)
r.xgroup_create("events", "processors", id="0", mkstream=True)

# Read up to 10 undelivered messages for this worker
msgs = r.xreadgroup("processors", "worker-1", {"events": ">"}, count=10, block=5000)
for _stream, entries in (msgs or []):
    for msg_id, data in entries:
        process(data)
        r.xack("events", "processors", msg_id)  # explicit ACK — like committing an offset

bytes.topic: Redis as Cache — TTL, eviction policies, cache-aside vs write-through vs write-behind
bytes.title: Kafka Consumer Enrichment with Redis — Cache-Aside, Write-Through, and Eviction Policies
bytes.body:
When Kafka consumers enrich events with external data — user profiles, product metadata, feature flags — hitting PostgreSQL on every message creates backpressure and lag. Redis absorbs that load, but the pattern you choose matters.

Cache-aside is the default for Kafka enrichment pipelines: on each polled message, check Redis first. On a miss, fetch from the DB and SET with a TTL (ex=300). Consumers stay stateless; stale reads are bounded by TTL. This is the right default for read-heavy, eventually-consistent enrichment.

Write-through makes sense when your Kafka topic is itself a mutation stream (e.g., user.updated, inventory.changed). The consumer writes to both DB and Redis synchronously inside the same message handler — cache stays warm, but you pay write latency on every event.

Write-behind buffers writes in Redis and flushes async. Dangerous with Kafka's at-least-once semantics: a rebalance or consumer crash before the async flush means replayed messages re-insert already-written data. You need idempotent DB writes to survive this.

Eviction policy: always run allkeys-lru on enrichment caches. noeviction will stall your consumer when Redis hits maxmemory — a silent throughput killer. Pair with consumer-group lag alerts to catch TTL-driven miss storms under high reprocessing load.
bytes.code_snippet:
from confluent_kafka import Consumer
import redis, json

r = redis.Redis(host="localhost", decode_responses=True)
consumer = Consumer({"bootstrap.servers": "localhost:9092", "group.id": "enrichment-svc", "auto.offset.reset": "earliest"})
consumer.subscribe(["order.created"])

def get_user_cached(user_id: str) -> dict:
    hit = r.get(f"user:{user_id}")
    if hit:
        return json.loads(hit)
    user = db_fetch(user_id)  # PostgreSQL call
    r.set(f"user:{user_id}", json.dumps(user), ex=300)  # cache-aside, 5min TTL
    return user

while True:
    msg = consumer.poll(timeout=1.0)
    if msg and not msg.error():
        event = json.loads(msg.value())
        user = get_user_cached(event["user_id"])
        process_order(event, user)

bytes.topic: Why Caching — reducing DB load, latency, cache-aside pattern
bytes.title: Kafka Consumers and Cache-Aside — Eliminating Per-Message DB Lookups
bytes.body:
Every Kafka consumer that enriches messages with reference data — user tiers, config flags, product metadata — faces a choice: query Postgres on every poll() or cache aggressively. At 50k messages/sec, uncached lookups saturate your DB connection pool in seconds.

Cache-aside is the right pattern here. On each consumed message, check Redis first. On a miss, query Postgres, write to Redis with a TTL, and continue. On a hit, zero DB traffic. With a 300-second TTL and a user base of 100k, your DB sees at most ~333 queries/min for user lookups instead of 3M+.

Key Kafka-specific consideration: consumer group rebalances don't invalidate your external Redis cache — it holds state independently of partition assignment, so a rebalance won't trigger a thundering herd. Contrast this with Kafka Streams' built-in KeyValueStore (RocksDB-backed), which is partition-local and changelog-replicated — better for stateful stream processing but overkill for simple enrichment.

Tune your cache TTL against message lag tolerance. A 5-min TTL on user tier data is safe if tier changes propagate through a separate user-updates topic that your consumer also subscribes to.
bytes.code_snippet:
from confluent_kafka import Consumer
import redis, json

cache = redis.Redis(host="localhost", port=6379, decode_responses=True)
consumer = Consumer({"bootstrap.servers": "broker:9092", "group.id": "order-enricher"})
consumer.subscribe(["raw-orders"])

while True:
    msg = consumer.poll(timeout=1.0)
    if not msg or msg.error(): continue
    order = json.loads(msg.value())
    uid = order["user_id"]
    if not (user := cache.get(f"user:{uid}")):
        user = fetch_from_postgres(uid)           # SELECT name, tier FROM users
        cache.setex(f"user:{uid}", 300, user)     # 5-min TTL, cache-aside write
    enrich_and_forward(order, json.loads(user))

bytes.topic: NATS — JetStream, subject-based routing, at-least-once delivery
bytes.title: Kafka to NATS JetStream — Subjects, Durables, and At-Least-Once Delivery
bytes.body:
Kafka veterans will find NATS JetStream familiar in intent but different in mechanics. Where Kafka uses topics with partitions, NATS uses subjects — dot-delimited strings like `orders.created` or `payments.failed`. JetStream streams are the persistence layer: you bind subject wildcards (`orders.>` captures any depth, `orders.*` captures one token) to a stream, analogous to a Kafka topic absorbing all partition writes.

At-least-once delivery maps directly to JetStream's `AckExplicit` policy — messages redeliver if unacknowledged within `AckWait`, equivalent to Kafka's `enable.auto.commit=false` with manual `commitSync()`. Durable consumers are the JetStream analog of Kafka consumer groups: they track sequence numbers (not byte offsets) and survive client restarts.

The sharpest difference: no partition concept. NATS scales via stream replicas (`Replicas: 3`) and subject filtering rather than sharding. Push consumers handle fan-out; pull consumers give you Kafka-style poll-loop semantics via `Fetch(batch, timeout)` for explicit backpressure control — useful when your downstream processor is rate-limited and you can't absorb unbounded push delivery.

bytes.topic: Backpressure — what happens when consumers lag, preventing memory blowout
bytes.title: Kafka Backpressure — Using pause() and resume() to Stop Consumer Memory Blowout
bytes.body:
When your Kafka consumer processes messages slower than the broker delivers them, unbounded in-memory buffering causes heap exhaustion. Lowering `max.poll.records` reduces per-batch size but doesn't stop a slow downstream from accumulating pressure over time.

The idiomatic Kafka solution is `KafkaConsumer.pause(Collection<TopicPartition>)`. Calling it on overloaded partitions causes the next `poll()` to return empty results for those partitions — critically, it still sends heartbeats, keeping you in the consumer group and avoiding a rebalance. When your downstream drains, call `resume(partitions)` to re-enable fetching.

Pair this with a bounded in-memory queue and two thresholds: a high-water mark (pause) and a low-water mark (resume). Hysteresis between the two prevents thrashing.

Also tune `max.poll.interval.ms` (default 5 min) to exceed your worst-case processing time. Breach it and the broker marks you dead, triggering a rebalance mid-batch.

Monitor consumer lag with `kafka-consumer-groups.sh --describe` or the `AdminClient` `listConsumerGroupOffsets` API — lag is your early warning before memory pressure becomes a crash.
bytes.code_snippet:
from confluent_kafka import Consumer
from queue import Queue

BUFFER_HIGH, BUFFER_LOW = 500, 100
buffer: Queue = Queue(maxsize=BUFFER_HIGH + 100)
paused = False

consumer = Consumer({"bootstrap.servers": "localhost:9092", "group.id": "svc-A"})
consumer.subscribe(["events"])

while True:
    msg = consumer.poll(timeout=1.0)
    if msg and not msg.error():
        buffer.put(msg)

    if not paused and buffer.qsize() >= BUFFER_HIGH:
        consumer.pause(consumer.assignment())
        paused = True
    elif paused and buffer.qsize() <= BUFFER_LOW:
        consumer.resume(consumer.assignment())
        paused = False

bytes.topic: BullMQ — job priorities, rate limiting, repeatable jobs, job dependencies
bytes.title: Kafka vs BullMQ — Offload Scheduled & Prioritized Jobs to Redis Instead
bytes.body:
Kafka excels at high-throughput event streaming, but it has no native concept of job priorities, per-consumer rate limits, or job-level dependencies — patterns that task queues need daily. BullMQ fills that gap using Redis as its backbone.

Priorities let you queue a 'critical' job at level 1 and a background report at level 10 — workers always drain higher-priority buckets first. Rate limiters throttle worker concurrency to N jobs per interval, ideal for wrapping third-party APIs behind your Kafka consumers without backpressure spilling upstream.

Repeatable jobs use a cron expression stored in Redis; unlike a Kafka topic with a retention window, they self-reschedule and skip missed runs cleanly. Job dependencies (parent/child flows) let you model pipelines: a parent job completes only when all its children succeed, giving you DAG semantics without a separate orchestrator.

When your Kafka consumer triggers work that needs retries, scheduling, or fan-out coordination, hand that work off to a BullMQ queue rather than re-publishing to another topic.

bytes.topic: Ordering — when order matters, partitioning strategy for ordered processing
bytes.title: Kafka Ordering — Partition Keys Are Your Only Guarantee
bytes.body:
Kafka guarantees message order strictly within a single partition — never across them. Consumers processing different partitions operate independently with no cross-partition sequencing. To enforce ordered processing for a logical entity (an order, a user session, a device), set that entity's ID as the message key. The producer runs the key through murmur2 and consistently routes it to the same partition: same key → same partition → FIFO delivery.

Watch for partition skew: a high-cardinality hotspot key (e.g., a power user) can starve other consumers. Use a compound key like `userId:shardBucket` and mod the bucket count to spread load while preserving intra-entity order.

For global ordering, use a single partition — but you sacrifice all consumer parallelism. That tradeoff almost never makes sense at scale. Instead, model your domain around per-entity ordering: order state machines, device telemetry, and user event streams all fit naturally.

Critically, set `enable.idempotence=true` and `max.in.flight.requests.per.connection=1` on the producer. Without idempotence, a retry on a failed batch can reorder messages within the same partition — silently breaking your ordering guarantee.
bytes.code_snippet:
from confluent_kafka import Producer

producer = Producer({
    "bootstrap.servers": "localhost:9092",
    "enable.idempotence": "true",
    "max.in.flight.requests.per.connection": "1",
    "acks": "all",
})

def publish_order_event(order_id: str, payload: bytes) -> None:
    # murmur2(order_id) → deterministic partition assignment
    producer.produce(topic="order-events", key=order_id.encode(), value=payload)
    producer.flush()

bytes.topic: Monitoring — queue depth alerts, consumer lag, dead letter queue alerts
bytes.title: Kafka Monitoring — Consumer Lag, Queue Depth, and Dead Letter Alerts That Actually Matter
bytes.body: Consumer lag is the most critical Kafka metric: it's the delta between the latest offset in a partition and the last committed offset of a consumer group. Fetch it via `kafka.consumer:type=consumer-fetch-manager-metrics,client-id=*` JMX beans, or use the AdminClient API with `listConsumerGroupOffsets()` diff'd against `listOffsets()` for each partition. Alert when lag exceeds your SLA buffer — typically 2–5× your normal processing throughput. For queue depth (log-end-offset), the AdminClient `listOffsets()` with `OffsetSpec.latest()` gives per-partition depth without consuming messages. Dead letter queues in Kafka are conventionally suffixed `.DLT` (Spring Kafka) or `-dlq`. Monitor DLT consumer lag separately — a growing DLT lag means poison pills are accumulating unprocessed. Wire these three signals into Prometheus via `kafka_exporter` (JMX exporter) and set Grafana alerts: lag > threshold for > 2 minutes triggers PagerDuty. Never alert on instantaneous spikes — use a sustained window to avoid noise from rebalances.

bytes.topic: Scaling — horizontal consumer scaling, partition count, rebalancing
bytes.title: Kafka Scaling — Partitions, Consumer Groups, and Rebalancing
bytes.body: Kafka's parallelism ceiling is your partition count. A consumer group assigns one partition per consumer — if you have 12 partitions and spin up 15 consumers, 3 sit idle. Plan partition count at topic creation; shrinking is unsupported and expanding triggers rebalancing. Rebalancing is Kafka's Achilles heel at scale: every time a consumer joins, leaves, or crashes, the group coordinator pauses all consumption, revokes assignments, and redistributes. With the default `RangeAssignor` and eager protocol, this causes a full stop-the-world pause. Switch to `CooperativeStickyAssignor` and the incremental cooperative rebalancing protocol — only partitions that need to move are revoked, keeping the rest consuming. Tune `max.poll.interval.ms` to match your actual processing time; exceeding it marks the consumer dead and triggers a rebalance. Use `session.timeout.ms` (default 45s) to control how quickly a crashed consumer is detected. For large groups, increase `group.initial.rebalance.delay.ms` so Kafka waits for all consumers to join before assigning, avoiding cascading rebalances at startup.

bytes.topic: Celery — task routing, beat scheduler, result backends, task chaining
bytes.title: Kafka Task Patterns — Routing, Beat Scheduling, Result Backends, and Chaining Without Celery
bytes.body:
Kafka replaces Celery's task abstractions with explicit topic-per-concern design. For task routing, dedicate a topic per task type (`email-tasks`, `report-tasks`) and assign separate consumer groups per worker pool — mirrors `task_routes` but gives partition-level parallelism you can scale without redeploying.

Celery Beat has no Kafka native equivalent. Use a Kubernetes CronJob that produces a trigger message to a `scheduled-tasks` topic on schedule. For in-stream scheduling, Kafka Streams' `punctuate()` API fires periodic logic inside a processor topology without an external scheduler.

Result backends: skip Redis. Produce results to a `task-results` topic keyed by `correlation_id`. Use a log-compacted topic so only the latest result per key is retained — callers poll by key with `auto.offset.reset=latest`. No TTL configuration needed.

Task chaining is Kafka's native strength: each processor consumes from one topic, transforms, and produces to the next, forming a linear DAG. With `enable.idempotence=true` and `acks=all`, every hop commits durably before the downstream consumer starts — stronger delivery guarantees than Celery's `chain()` primitive, which can silently drop on broker restart.
bytes.code_snippet:
from confluent_kafka import Producer, Consumer
import json, uuid

p = Producer({'bootstrap.servers': 'localhost:9092', 'enable.idempotence': True, 'acks': 'all'})

def dispatch(task_type: str, payload: dict) -> str:
    cid = str(uuid.uuid4())
    p.produce(f"{task_type}-tasks", key=cid, value=json.dumps(payload))
    p.flush()
    return cid  # correlation_id — poll task-results topic by this key

def chain_worker(src: str, dst: str, fn):
    c = Consumer({'bootstrap.servers': 'localhost:9092',
                  'group.id': f'{src}-group', 'auto.offset.reset': 'earliest'})
    c.subscribe([src])
    while True:
        msg = c.poll(1.0)
        if msg and not msg.error():
            p.produce(dst, key=msg.key(), value=json.dumps(fn(json.loads(msg.value()))))
            p.flush()

bytes.topic: Security — broker authentication, TLS, network isolation
bytes.title: Kafka Security — SASL, TLS, and Listener Isolation Done Right
bytes.body:
Kafka brokers support four SASL mechanisms: PLAIN, SCRAM-SHA-256/512, GSSAPI (Kerberos), and OAUTHBEARER. For most cloud deployments, SCRAM-SHA-512 over SASL_SSL is the practical default — it avoids plaintext credential exposure without Kerberos infrastructure complexity. TLS requires ssl.keystore.location and ssl.truststore.location on both brokers and clients; use PEM-formatted certs with ssl.keystore.type=PEM (Kafka 2.7+) to skip JKS entirely.

Listener isolation is non-negotiable: define separate listeners for internal broker-to-broker traffic and external client traffic. Set listeners=INTERNAL://0.0.0.0:9093,EXTERNAL://0.0.0.0:9094 with listener.security.protocol.map=INTERNAL:PLAINTEXT,EXTERNAL:SASL_SSL. Advertised listeners must match listener names exactly or clients will fail to bootstrap with a confusing "Broker not available" error.

Pair authentication with ACLs: kafka-acls.sh --add --allow-principal User:service-account --operation Read --topic payments grants topic-level access. Set authorizer.class.name=kafka.security.authorizer.AclAuthorizer and super.users=User:admin in server.properties. Without ACLs, authenticated users can still read any topic — SASL proves identity, ACLs enforce access. One without the other is half a lock.
bytes.code_snippet:
from confluent_kafka import Producer

conf = {
    'bootstrap.servers': 'broker:9094',
    'security.protocol': 'SASL_SSL',
    'sasl.mechanism': 'SCRAM-SHA-512',
    'sasl.username': 'service-account',
    'sasl.password': 's3cr3t',
    'ssl.ca.location': '/etc/kafka/certs/ca.pem',
    'ssl.endpoint.identification.algorithm': 'https',
}

producer = Producer(conf)
producer.produce('payments', key='txn-1', value='{"amount": 100}')
producer.flush()

bytes.topic: Testing — testing consumers in isolation, mocking brokers in tests
bytes.title: Kafka MockConsumer — Testing Consumers Without a Running Broker
bytes.body:
Testing Kafka consumers in isolation avoids broker overhead and keeps tests deterministic. The `kafka-clients` library ships a `MockConsumer<K, V>` that implements the full `Consumer<K, V>` interface — inject it instead of `KafkaConsumer` and control exactly what records your consumer sees.

Key setup: call `assign()` to bind partitions manually, seed offsets with `updateBeginningOffsets()`, then push records via `addRecord(new ConsumerRecord<>(topic, partition, offset, key, value))`. No ZooKeeper, no broker, sub-millisecond feedback.

For integration-level tests where real serialization, offset commits, and consumer group rebalancing matter, Spring Kafka's `@EmbeddedKafka` spins up an in-process broker on a random port. Annotate the test class with `@EmbeddedKafka(partitions = 1, topics = {"orders"})` and inject `EmbeddedKafkaBroker` to get the bootstrap address wired into your `KafkaTemplate` and listener containers.

Unit tests with `MockConsumer` catch deserialization bugs and business logic errors fast. Integration tests with `@EmbeddedKafka` catch offset management gaps and serialization round-trip failures. The two layers cover non-overlapping failure modes — run both.
bytes.code_snippet:
val mockConsumer = MockConsumer<String, String>(OffsetResetStrategy.EARLIEST)
val partition = TopicPartition("orders", 0)
mockConsumer.assign(listOf(partition))
mockConsumer.updateBeginningOffsets(mapOf(partition to 0L))
mockConsumer.addRecord(
    ConsumerRecord("orders", 0, 0L, "k1", """{"id":1,"amount":99}""")
)

val orderService = mockk<OrderService>()
OrderConsumer(mockConsumer, orderService).pollOnce()

verify { orderService.process(match { it.id == 1 && it.amount == 99 }) }

bytes.topic: When to Use What — Redis vs Kafka vs RabbitMQ decision framework
bytes.title: Kafka vs RabbitMQ vs Redis — A Decision Framework Built Around Kafka's Strengths
bytes.body:
Kafka wins when you need replayability and fan-out: multiple independent consumer groups each maintain their own offsets in the internal `__consumer_offsets` topic, so a billing service and an analytics pipeline can independently consume the same `order-created` topic without interfering. RabbitMQ deletes messages after acknowledgment — no replay, no audit trail. Redis Pub/Sub drops messages entirely if no subscriber is connected at publish time.

Choose RabbitMQ when your workload is task-queue shaped — short-lived jobs, complex routing via exchanges (direct, topic, fanout, headers), and per-message TTL or dead-letter queues without manual Kafka topic plumbing.

Choose Redis Streams when latency is critical (sub-millisecond), data is ephemeral, and throughput fits in memory. `XADD`/`XREADGROUP` gives consumer-group semantics without broker overhead, but durability is limited to your Redis persistence config.

Kafka is the right call for high-throughput event sourcing: set `retention.ms=-1` for infinite log retention, `log.cleanup.policy=compact` for stateful key-based topics, and `enable.idempotence=true` on the producer for exactly-once delivery per partition. Partition count is your parallelism lever — size it to your peak consumer group concurrency.
bytes.code_snippet:
from confluent_kafka import Consumer, Producer

# Idempotent producer: exactly-once delivery per partition
producer = Producer({
    "bootstrap.servers": "localhost:9092",
    "enable.idempotence": True,
    "acks": "all",
})

# Independent consumer group: replay without affecting other services
consumer = Consumer({
    "bootstrap.servers": "localhost:9092",
    "group.id": "billing-service",
    "auto.offset.reset": "earliest",   # replay from log start
    "enable.auto.commit": False,        # manual commit for at-least-once
})

consumer.subscribe(["order-created"])
msg = consumer.poll(1.0)
if msg and not msg.error():
    process(msg.value())
    consumer.commit()  # persisted to __consumer_offsets
